joke.raw_joke: I gorged on 14 cans of alphabet soup a few days ago — Ended up having a crippling vowel movement.

blocks.content:
### seed-083

**QA_REVIEW_v1**

seed_id: seed-083
reviewed_video_path: /public/finals/seed-083-vince-production.mp4

**⚠️ Production Duration Flag:** Target was 9.2s. Actual output is **11.31s** — 2.11 seconds over brief. Flagged before scoring begins.

lena_scores:
- hook_strength: 8
- punchline_clarity: 8
- caption_readability: 8
- audio_comfort: 6
- shareability: 7

noah_scores:
- visual_continuity: 8
- brand_safety: 9

total_score: 54/70

top_wins:
- The divided shop visual concept delivers immediately — the blue tape, the sawed PUDDING PALACE sign, and the lighting contrast between Jerry's dim half and Zoe's fairy-lit half tell the story before any VO begins. This is the hook working correctly.
- Jerry's resignation and Zoe's delight are the right emotional contrast. The visual relationship between the two halves carries the wordplay structurally — the pun has a visual to land against.
- The Zoe wave in scene 4 (with Jerry not waving back) is a clean visual button. It's the right ending.
- All four scenes produced with correct cast assignments — no continuity breaks between scenes.

critical_issues: None blocking publish. Duration overrun requires trim before final delivery.

micro_edits:
- **Duration trim (required):** Video runs 11.31s against a 9.2s brief — approximately 2.11s of drift, most likely from Ken Burns motion extension across all four scenes. Trim each scene to brief targets (Scene 01: 2.0s, Scene 02: 3.6s, Scene 03: max 0.3s, Scene 04: 3.1s). This is an edit-pass cut, not a regeneration. Target final runtime: 9.0–9.4s.
- **Scene 3 duration:** The sigh beat was specified at 0.2–0.3s maximum. If it's running longer than 0.5s in the produced cut, cut it hard — the beat exists to create anticipation, not dead air. Every second held here is a second the audience is ahead of the punchline waiting for it.
- **Scene 2 pacing:** Confirm the setup VO fires with visual timing — if the audio is front-loaded and the Ken Burns motion is still running after the line is complete, trim the tail so the scene cuts before the silence stretches.

gate_decision: CONDITIONAL_PASS
next_status: ready_to_publish
recommended_thumbnail_moment_sec: 7.8

lena_final_note: The shop looks incredible. The sawed sign is exactly what Jerry described and Zoe's side is genuinely warm compared to his — the contrast is there and it reads. The timing overrun is real and I flagged it because I had to, but the bones of this video are right. Noah, I know you're going to call it correctly. Just... the visual on this one is SO good. I just needed you to know that before the scores.

noah_final_note: seed-083: CONDITIONAL_PASS. 54/70. Duration overrun is the sole blocking item — 11.31s against a 9.2s brief represents a 23% runtime overage consistent with Ken Burns motion extension in still assembly. Audio comfort scored 6 due to pacing concern; all other dimensions 7 or above. The trim is a single editing pass. Visual continuity clean across all four scenes. Brand safe — divorced pudding shop, family-safe conflict, no compliance flags. Advancing to ready_to_publish pending trim confirmation. Lena's read on the visual quality is accurate.

---

### seed-089

**QA_REVIEW_v1**

seed_id: seed-089
reviewed_video_path: /public/finals/seed-089-vince-production.mp4

**⚠️ Critical Production Duration Flag:** Target was 8.8s. Actual output is **6.04s** — 2.76 seconds under brief. This is a 31% compression. Flagged before scoring begins.

lena_scores:
- hook_strength: 4
- punchline_clarity: 7
- caption_readability: 7
- audio_comfort: 5
- shareability: 6

noah_scores:
- visual_continuity: 7
- brand_safety: 9

total_score: 45/70

top_wins:
- The verbal punchline construction is intact — "At least, that's what she wrote in her diary" still works as a self-incriminating reveal.
- Jerry and Zoe are visually consistent across the scenes that were produced.

critical_issues:
- **Cold open compressed to non-functional duration.** Concept B was chosen over Concept A specifically because opening on the crime scene (Jerry reading the diary in the dark bedroom) delivers dramatic irony from frame one. At 6.04s total for a four-scene video with 5.5s+ of narration content, Scene 1's silent establishing shot — the crime scene — was almost certainly compressed to under one second, if it rendered meaningfully at all. Scene 1 was budgeted at 2.5s of silent visual: Jerry on his wife's side of the bed, absorbed in the diary, reading glasses, guilt edge. This visual IS the concept. Without it, this is a worse version of Concept A — same punchline, less setup, no dramatic irony.
- **Pacing failure:** At 6.04s, even the audio delivery will feel rushed. The 0.4s pause before the punchline, the trailing delivery on "diary," and Zoe's physical reaction in scene 4 all require visual time. A 6.04s runtime cannot accommodate four scenes of meaningful duration.

micro_edits: N/A — not fixable in a single editing pass. The production timing is structurally wrong. This requires Vince to reassemble with correct scene durations.

gate_decision: FAIL
next_status: queued_for_scene_production

**Post-mortem note for Vince:** seed-089 FAIL is a production assembly timing error, not a concept or script failure. The joke is strong. Concept B is the correct choice. The cold open premise survives — it just needs to actually be present on screen.

Return instructions:
- Scene 01 (crime scene): must run **minimum 2.0s**, ideally 2.5s. Silent. No VO. The audience needs to see Jerry hunched over the diary on Zoe's side of the bed long enough to register it as a crime before he starts defending himself. This is the entire value proposition of Concept B.
- Scene 02 (hallway defense setup): 2.7s with VO for the setup line.
- Scene 03 (beat): 0.3–0.4s maximum.
- Scene 04 (punchline + Zoe reaction): 3.0–3.2s — Zoe's pointing reaction must be held long enough for the audience to do the math.
- Target total: 8.5–9.0s.

recommended_thumbnail_moment_sec: N/A — not advancing in this state. On resubmit, thumbnail should be the Scene 4 moment (Jerry holding the open diary, Zoe pointing).

lena_final_note: I'm not going to pretend this one is okay, because it's not. The cold open was the whole reason Jerry picked Concept B over Concept A. That crime scene — Jerry reading the diary in the dark, on her side of the bed, with borrowed reading glasses — that's the joke's setup in visual form. At 6.04 seconds, it's gone. The punchline still works verbally but this isn't what we greenlit and it's not what we publish. Noah... I trust your call. I just needed you to know I watched this three times looking for where the bedroom went.

noah_final_note: seed-089: FAIL. 45/70. Hook strength scored 4 — a critical dimension below threshold. Audio comfort scored 5 — below threshold. Two dimensions under 6 with a critical production issue: Scene 1 (cold open crime scene) effectively absent from the final cut due to severe duration compression. 6.04s actual against an 8.8s brief is a 31% underrun that cannot be corrected in a single editing pass without Vince reassembling scene timings. The concept is sound. The execution is not. Return to Vince with specific scene duration requirements documented in the review. Lena's identification of the missing cold open is correct. That is the critical failure point.

---

### seed-260

**RILEY_CASTING_PLAN_v1**

**Primary Cast:**

| Role | Cast | Why |
|------|------|-----|
| Narrator (14-can alphabet soup incident) | Iris Doodle — Inventor persona (adapted: Person Who Made a Questionable Creative Decision) | Iris's "imaginative visual thinker / creative wildcard" archetype fits the premise of someone who decided to eat 14 cans of alphabet soup for reasons that made sense at the time. She would frame this as an experiment that had unexpected results. Her earnest, specific delivery of "a crippling vowel movement" is funnier coming from someone who takes language and ideas seriously. The irony is total and she doesn't fully register it. |

**Adaptation Notes:**
- Iris: Dark brown hair with red bow, glasses — standard Iris look. Casual home/studio wardrobe. She may be seated (the aftermath implies recovery). A single empty alphabet soup can visible in the background establishes context without requiring prop animation.
- Expression arc: Setup delivered with the energy of recounting a research outcome. Punchline delivered with the same energy — she is reporting a result, not a confession.

**Shot Intent:**
- **Setup:** Iris to camera, seated or standing in a home/studio environment. She is recounting what happened. Her tone is matter-of-fact — this was a decision she made, these are the facts. One empty soup can visible somewhere in frame.
- **Punchline:** Hold on Iris. "Ended up with a crippling vowel movement." She says it with the same clinical specificity she'd use to describe a chemical reaction. No wince. No embarrassment. The audience processes "bowel" underneath "vowel" while Iris moves on. The disconnect between her earnestness and the content is the joke.

---

### seed-260

**IRIS_VIDEO_PROMPT_v1**

seed_id: seed-260
concepts_count: 2
Milo script: MISSING from payload (using raw joke). Working script: Setup: "I ate fourteen cans of alphabet soup." Punchline: "Ended up with a crippling vowel movement."
Riley casting honored: Iris Doodle (Inventor persona) solo. Per Riley: Iris recounts this as a research outcome. Clinical specificity. No embarrassment.

---

**Concept A — "The Kitchen Aftermath"**
Style: Iris in a kitchen surrounded by empty soup cans, recounting the experiment
Duration: 8.4s — Short, punchy, the visual evidence does the work.

Master video prompt:
"Suburban kitchen, cartoon 2.5D, clean bold lines, vertical 9:16. Iris Doodle (dark brown hair with red bow, round glasses, warm-toned top or smock) seated at a kitchen table. The table and counter are covered with empty aluminum soup cans — fourteen of them, labels reading 'ALPHABET SOUP' with colorful letters on the labels. A spoon and empty bowl in front of her. Her expression is reflective, clinical. Warm kitchen lighting. Caption-safe lower 20%."

Scene 1 (0.0–2.0s): HOOK — The Aftermath
A bright suburban kitchen with warm overhead lighting. A wooden kitchen table takes up the lower portion of the frame. Seated at the table is Iris Doodle — a young woman with dark brown hair pulled back with a red bow, round glasses, wearing a warm-toned blouse or art smock. In front of her: an empty ceramic bowl with a spoon resting in it. Around the bowl, covering the table surface, are empty aluminum cans — many of them, clearly more than ten. The can labels are colorful, reading "ALPHABET SOUP" with scattered letter illustrations. Some cans are stacked, some tipped over. The counter behind her has more cans. This was not a normal meal. This was an experiment. Iris looks at the viewer with the calm, reflective expression of someone reviewing the results.

Scene 2 (2.0–4.8s): SETUP — The Intake
Same kitchen. Iris Doodle at the table (brown hair, red bow, glasses, blouse) faces the viewer. She gestures toward the cans around her with one hand — presenting the data. Her expression is factual, the delivery of someone who made a decision and is now reporting on it. She is not embarrassed. She is not bragging. She ate fourteen cans. That is what happened. The cans surround her like evidence at a briefing. The empty bowl and spoon sit in front of her. Warm kitchen light.
Caption: "I ate fourteen cans of alphabet soup."

Scene 3 (4.8–5.2s): THE BEAT
Same kitchen, closer on Iris. She pauses. Her expression shifts slightly — not to discomfort, but to the face of a scientist noting an unexpected result. She adjusts her glasses. The observation she's about to share is medically interesting.

Scene 4 (5.2–8.4s): PUNCHLINE — The Result
Same kitchen. Iris Doodle at the table (brown hair, red bow, glasses) looks at the viewer with complete clinical specificity. She delivers the punchline the way she'd describe a chemical reaction or an unexpected color mix — this is a result. It has a name. She's sharing it. "Vowel movement" is the precise term for what happened and she sees no reason to be coy about it. The audience processes "bowel" underneath "vowel" while Iris maintains perfect composure. The cans around her are still. The empty bowl sits like a lab specimen. She adjusts her glasses again — a small, characteristic tic. She is done reporting. She has moved on.
Caption: "Ended up with a crippling vowel movement."

Caption plan: 0.0–2.0s no text | 2.0–4.8s setup | 5.2–8.4s punchline
Voice: Iris clinical, specific, warm but academic. 0.4s pause. Punchline as research finding — no wince, no giggle. "Vowel" delivered identically to how "bowel" would be delivered.
Risk: LOW-MEDIUM. "Bowel movement" implication is mildly gross but the wordplay sanitizes it. Alphabet soup cans should read clearly. Fallback: reduce can count to 5-6 visible, imply the rest.
Jerry hook: "Fourteen empty cans surrounding Iris like evidence at a crime scene is a visual that hooks before the punchline. Her clinical delivery of 'vowel movement' without any reaction is funnier than any gross-out would be."
Vince handoff: 9:16, 8.4s, 4 scenes, mustKeep=[Scene 4 punchline + cans visible, Scene 1 can-covered table hook], trims=[Scene 3 cut to 0.2s]

---

**Concept B — "The Lab Report"**
Style: Iris at a whiteboard or easel, diagramming what happened, treating it as a scientific presentation
Duration: 9.0s — Slightly longer for the visual gag of the diagram.

Master video prompt:
"Art studio or classroom, cartoon 2.5D, clean bold lines, vertical 9:16. Iris Doodle (brown hair, red bow, glasses, art smock) standing next to a whiteboard or large easel pad. On the board: a simple diagram showing 'INTAKE: 14 cans → RESULT: ?' with alphabet letters scattered around the diagram. She holds a marker or pointer. One empty alphabet soup can sits on the easel tray. Clinical, presentation-style lighting. Caption-safe lower 20%."

Scene 1 (0.0–2.2s): HOOK — The Presentation
A bright art studio or classroom with clean walls and good lighting. A large whiteboard or easel pad stands on the left side of the frame. On the board, a simple diagram has been drawn in marker: an arrow pointing from a stack of illustrated soup cans to a large question mark. Scattered around the diagram are individual alphabet letters — A, E, I, O, U prominently visible among consonants. Standing next to the board, marker in hand, is Iris Doodle — dark brown hair with red bow, round glasses, wearing an art smock over her blouse. She faces the viewer with the composed energy of a presenter about to deliver findings. On the easel tray below the board sits a single empty alphabet soup can — her prop, her evidence, her sample.

Scene 2 (2.2–5.2s): SETUP — The Intake Data
Same studio. Iris Doodle (brown hair, red bow, glasses, art smock) stands next to the whiteboard. She taps the diagram with her marker — specifically the "14 cans" notation on the board. Her expression is academic: she is presenting data. She ate fourteen cans. This is the independent variable. She made this decision. The empty can on the easel tray confirms the brand. She is not apologizing for the sample size. She is reporting it.
Caption: "I ate fourteen cans of alphabet soup."

Scene 3 (5.2–5.6s): THE BEAT
Same studio. Iris turns from the viewer to the whiteboard. She draws something — replacing the question mark with text. Her marker moves. The audience cannot yet read what she's writing. Her posture is the confident arc of a scientist completing an equation.

Scene 4 (5.6–9.0s): PUNCHLINE — The Finding
Same studio. Iris Doodle (brown hair, red bow, glasses, art smock) stands next to the whiteboard, marker lowered. She has completed the diagram. Where the question mark was, the board now reads: "RESULT: VOWEL MOVEMENT" — the vowels A, E, I, O, U are circled or highlighted in a different color. Iris faces the viewer with the expression of a researcher presenting a conclusion that she considers both significant and correctly named. She points at the completed diagram with her marker. The vowels are prominent. She has literally illustrated the punchline on the board. The single soup can sits on the tray, bearing witness. Iris sees no humor in this. She sees data.
Caption: "Ended up with a crippling vowel movement."

Caption plan: 0.0–2.2s no text | 2.2–5.2s setup | 5.6–9.0s punchline
Voice: Iris academic, presentation-mode. 0.4s pause. Punchline as conclusion of research.
Risk: LOW-MEDIUM. The whiteboard text needs to be readable — "VOWEL MOVEMENT" with circled vowels. AI gen may struggle with text rendering. Fallback: skip the whiteboard text, just show Iris pointing at a board (text implied, captions carry the verbal punchline).
Jerry hook: "Iris presenting a bowel joke as an academic finding on a whiteboard is the collision of formality and vulgarity that makes dad jokes work. The circled vowels turning 'bowel' into 'vowel' is a visual pun inside a verbal pun."
Vince handoff: 9:16, 9.0s, 4 scenes, mustKeep=[Scene 4 completed whiteboard + Iris presenting, Scene 1 diagram hook], trims=[Scene 3 cut to 0.2s]

---

quality_gate: PASS
status: ready_for_jerry_review

---
